proactive-context: single Rust binary, local fastembed + sqlite-vec, zero cloud.

No summarizer in the loop.

Honest answer first, because you asked a real question: as a **bag of features**, no — and verbatim-citation isn't even close to enough to carry it. The research bears this out. Every individual piece (hook-based capture, wiki substrate, local-first, learn-from-corrections, even verbatim injection) is matched somewhere. The red-team agent scored the feature-set a 2/10 and was right to.

So if there's something special, it can't be a *feature*. It has to be **what the thing is for** — a different theory of the job. And I think you've been pointing at it the whole time; I just kept reaching for the mechanism instead of the meaning.

Here's the reframe I'd actually bet on:

**Everyone is building tools to produce more model output. This is built to capture the one thing the model can't produce — your judgment.**

In a world where fluent output is now infinite, free, and commoditized, the scarce and *appreciating* asset is the human's discernment: your taste, your "no, not like that," your sense of what matters. That's relevance realization — the thing a model fundamentally doesn't have, because nothing matters *to* it. Almost the entire AI industry is pointed at generating more of the cheap, replaceable part. Vanishingly little is built to catch and compound the precious part.

That's the inversion. The model is the disposable input. **Your perspective is the asset.** This is infrastructure for the asset.

And *that's* where verbatim-citation finally earns its place — not as the headline, but as the **proof the system means it**. You can't claim to treasure someone's perspective and then run it through a summarizer; the paraphrase is the tell that you thought of it as disposable data all along. Verbatim is just what sincerity looks like. Same with the deep wiki (nuance gets room to live) and local-first (your judgment stays yours). They're all downstream of the one stance.

Three tweets in that vein, as a probe before I do all 16:

---

**A.** The scarce resource in 2026 isn't intelligence. It's someone who knows what *good* is.

Every time you tell an AI "no — like this," that's the asset. Everything else it can regenerate for free.

I built the thing that catches those moments and never makes you spend one twice.

---

**B.** We have it backwards.

We treat the model's output as precious and the human's corrections as friction to get through.

It's the opposite. The output is infinite and free. The correction is the only part that can't be regenerated.

proactive-context is built around that one fact.

---

**C.** Your taste doesn't compound. It evaporates — one closed session at a time.

Every preference, every overruled default, every "actually, no" is judgment a model will never have on its own. And it's gone the moment the chat ends.

This keeps it. Local, in your words, forever.

---

Is *that* the vein — "your judgment is the asset; the machine is the cheap part"? If it lands, I'll give you 16 across the full range of styles built on it. If it's still not it, tell me where it's off and I'll keep digging — it's possible the real thing is something I still haven't named.
